What that means is it makes Perfect sense That's what an airtight logical case does no ifs it just makes sense so the mistake that most salespeople make when they go about doing this is they don't spend time Walking in the customer's shoes They don't do that for that backwards looking thing where you say, okay, if I was that person Based on where they are in life right now based on what their business is with what their position is What would they want to hear? logically To be able to make the right decision. Okay, give you an example the trap is That you base Your airtight logical case in terms of that your product is really fairly priced Because price is a big thing right price is a very part price is part of your listen It's always gonna come down partly the price because we have what's called a cost benefit ratio And you have a value proposition What these things are essentially a cost benefit is in other words based on the price I have to pay and The benefits I'm gonna get well my cost is gonna be you know $10 and my benefits at work $100. I have 10 to 1 ratio. That's a very good cost benefit ratio, right? Conversely, I'm using very simple numbers here conversely if the cost of my I'm charging $100 or something and Value perspective is perceived value to the prospect is gonna be 75 dollars That's not a very good cause it's a negative cost benefit ratio. It's not gonna tip in your favor So one of the things that when I'm Making an airtight logical case. I'm always focusing in on the cost benefit ratio Essentially, what am I gonna charge this person for the product? good or service and based on that what are the actual tangible Benefits to this person dollar-wise There's what what what can they make off of my product now for instance when I did this training a few weeks back for this company I was in the digital digital marketing company, right? Then the fatal mistake they were making they still doing well by the way you with it is they were trying to Explain their product and I'll just put I'm gonna put an arbitrary value at a thousand dollars Thousand dollars spent right? When they were trying to explain to their prospects Why the product was priced at a thousand dollars? They were using the fact that it cost them The cost of producing it Was almost a thousand dollars Like we're not making a lot of money here. That's why it cost a thousand dollars and as they went about building this case And this is the case they were making and it worked just you know in a low level not nearly as well when you do the right way But the point was is they were making this case thing we offer this feature That has that benefit this feature does that for this thing like four things, right? And they stack them all there's a now and here's the price for the program And they say the price is x dollars and let me tell you why we price it that way Because it takes us x number of man hours. We had to write all the code and I'm like Who gives a fuck? That's not that's not an airtight logical case for anything. That's how you build an airtight logical case It's based on Essentially that you connect all the dots for someone in a slow deliberate fashion Without skipping over and it knows there's no sleight of hand involved sleight of hand involved is an emotional case I just imagine you know, you know, it's even an emotional case You don't have to consider all the different spaces a look you can say just imagine Six months from now sitting back in your chair with your business just put it fucking just spitting out money You just feel so great about it. Honestly, that's a lot emotional You don't have to connect the dots for that The problem though is that if you don't first connect up the dots and you try to just say that to someone They're like, oh, yeah bullshit bullshit the human mind has a bullshit detector because there's no logic involved So we always want to have the logical underpinning that we create and by doing that It opens someone up to be moved emotionally Let me give you a whole different perspective on this right for answers back and we change like doing another way you do it, right? back in the day the In the stock market, right? You have a thesis of why something makes sense So the idea was that Kodak and always comes down to math Most of logical cases right as we really comes out there, right? So it's Kodak is currently trading at $42 a share, right? and our thesis was is that Kodak Is going to a hundred plus that the stock is worth well over a hundred and it's going there over the short term Let me tell you why That's so that's how it's essentially here's where it is right now Here's what we think it's worth. Let me tell you the logical reasons why Logical sober reasons why now there's many ways to do this Okay, one way would be now right now Kodak earns four dollars a share that's its earnings right and in the stock market you have what's called a price Earnings ratio which means that based on certain into the consumer products might trade 20 times earnings You might have Come to the high tech trades 30 times earnings the banks trade at 10 times Everyone trades a certain multiple times this number so back in the days you'd say the entire Consumer products in this he trades at 25 times turn earnings. Why is the sock so cheap? Well, here's the story, okay? Right now Kodak is being sued by Polaroid for patent infringement and What happens is in the stock when a company like that gets sued by another big company the mass suit the stock drops like a rock in Fact, I'll tell you story remember back in 1985 when a plant from Union call by exploded in both Palinthia Killed like 15,000 Indians the stock went from 113 down to three dollars a share a disaster Guess what the day that lawsuit settled the stock run back up to 97 dollars people who bought the bottom made a fortune US Steel five years later was sued by Nippon Steel Okay, the day that company got sued the stock from 72 down to 16 people bailed out Okay, people have bought at 16 when the litigation settled the stock ran back up to 91 dollars here another huge winner Texaco got sued by Pennzoil again for infringement Okay, the day that Texio got sued by Pennzoil the stock went from 142 down to and I'm making up numbers guys We don't want them down to 19 Guess what I'm doing that litigation settled stock ran back to 110 Now let me say this right now Kodak so what I'm saying is there I'm I'm I'm stacking I'm painting the picture saying in the past Which is typically predictive of the future These big companies when they get caught up in major litigation the knee-jerk reaction the stock drops like a rock But what happens the smart money? I say you I'll get you to the front page of Google, right? Okay, kind of like the how like the logic is a why is that so crucial and without dragging on a Little tidbit of just how we do that One of the true beauties we do here sir is that we have people employed that are experts in this area and through strategic But and whatever it might doesn't matter what is I come on talk boom boom boom boom a couple of words Okay, it allows you to pop up to page one and because of that Buying habits are that when someone goes in on their smartphone or home on the desktop Okay, they typically look at the first seven statistics say the first seven things they stop and you fill in the blanks there Why it's important the airtight logical case is about the why so it's not so much like your your first case is That you're so you frame a case right you frame a logical case and it's about one thing and then when you Build an airtight case. It's just more it's separate things, but just more logic. That's not what it is It's the same thing that you started with but a deeper understanding of it And that's where most people fall down They're not building a picture for someone they jump because just more arcane facts on top of more arcane facts And after a while people just say it's just they're overwhelmed versus allowing people to develop a very detailed understanding of Why something is a good deal? Essentially of how that is a you know how walls but not but not to a point You're giving them a crazy words that just you know a flying over the heads But it's sort of the how and why of why something is truly a great deal Why it makes perfect empirical sense and you say it to them in a way Using in some industry jargon, but you're not talking over someone's head see the last thing you want to do is Talk to someone in a series of techno babble and they don't understand what you're saying like I don't get it What you want to do the really smart people are able of straight story tells are able to explain something to someone in a way that sounds very sophisticated, but it's actually very easy to understand So when you're creating your scripts and you're developing your language patterns You want to use just enough technical industry jargon that you sound like you know You know you know you're an expert in your field, but not so much That people like what the fuck is this guy talking about? I don't get it It's just like it's no old man and they tune out they almost like almost offended by they feel you trying to cover up something Or you simply just not an effective communicator and they don't buy from you You get it so you want to use the power of Language to speak just about their head like you know if someone like watch imagine a scale We are so he's nice down video right so now this is a one a One year instead of ten year right and this is like when you speak in layman's terms Okay, we're like it you know totally novice right this is where an educated person would be able to understand right and this is where an expert Their language you want to be right around here like a seven Above the level that someone like a small person you want to be a little bit above that but not over here This is techno wizards bullshit and people tune out That's also part of developing an airtight logical case now remember Okay, one of the keys here is we need to stack That's the word I want you to think about when you're creating your scripts, okay Remember that you're the idea is that you want to have sort of three bites at the apple You're not gonna look to write this perfect unborn Denialy logical pattern that's so great that when you're done the prospects like fuck that was so great I just see no reason not to buy it doesn't work that way no matter what you do But how hard you try to create that level of certainty it doesn't happen all at once in one shot It takes more okay, but I gotta shut this fire guys one second It's getting really hot yet it's got hot right so A little fireman Doesn't work so what you do is you you want to sort of take three Bites at the apple so you want to have these three a sort of you know You make up your first one is the main body of your presentation Your second bite is represented by your first loop And your third bite is represented by the next loop so watch how it plays out here on the straight line so watch Open Close this is your perfect sale right here These are your boundaries above and below the line You have your first four seconds where you capture their attention position as an expert you want to end up intelligence Right gi build massive rapport you do your straight line transition right and now you're gonna start by framing It's logical case that goes to about here So in other words, you're still a lot of not like halfway down. It's a lot of this is the frame And again the best metaphor is look at the frame of the house you lay the foundation You put up the the overall the wood frame, but you don't put up the drywall the like the in the electricity the plumbing That comes later So what you got to do is when you when you you could write Your whole when you're building the script you can write the whole thing out in one shot But then you got to look at okay. What am I dividing points here? What am I what's what's my best most logical way so I can you know say X and then ask for the order for this Watch so you want to have your initial language pattern Take you to here and then you're gonna ask for the order for the first time. This is your first close right here To give me one shot and believe me you'll be very very impressed sound fair enough That's right, and then you're gonna either close the deal But more likely you'll get hit with your first objection right he says let me think about it So he goes off the line here. So you say I hear what you stop them say I hear what you're saying But let me ask you a question. Does the idea make sense to you do you like the idea right and he says yeah It sounds pretty good, and now you're gonna owe such a loop back on the line into presentation mode and you pick up at the exact point That you left off in your initial presentation. This is the key here these these Presentations need to connect to one another Your first print your initial presentation and your first looping pattern. And you divide them up over three presentations Each presentation have no more than three core points to it Any more than that it becomes noise You get it so five people you have a bunch you get to pull out 15 of them Well, you pull out nine of them, right and you use those nine as your core points Then you have some others you can use afterwards as backups, right? You're you're on the level You're on the level it makes sense Once they think that The rest becomes pretty easy All right now now in the real let's not remember this okay in the real here's my one final thought here because it's very important Right in the real world of selling now While you only have to have three patterns it actually behooves you to have more like five Because remember not all customers will create equal They have different needs different beliefs different values different pain points So I want you to have some extra backup patterns as well For when the sale drags on because if at any point along the way You're running out of intelligence things to say you start saying stupid shit You break reports over so you need to have your backup patterns as well Three core patterns you have three patterns for product Three patterns for yourself Three patterns for your company and those three patterns are designed as structured to build upon each other But you want to have your backups as well and also if you're in you know, it's like I guess it's like The more the merrier but only if you need them In other words, you want to have these reserve patterns here that sound great that makes sense What you don't want to do is under prepare yourself And get caught saying stupid shit I'll go back to that sale that I made that day in what it was a mock sale right where I was being filmed right The ability what would close that deal Was my ability to keep talking and talking and talking without putting my foot in the mouth I already I kind of got the guy 15 or 18 minutes in the last four minutes was sort of my ability to keep going and get it using that You know to really kind of box them in but without Doing something that would sort of say ah, he's full of shit That's why you need to be able to go a bit lower. So you want to have your extra patterns in there just to safety's sake But really in reality those three patterns 80 90s and those will do the trick Now remember logical certainty opens up the possibility And emotional certainty is what closes the prospect and emotional certainty is based on two things number one The minor part is your ability to use tonality To get someone excited So through the process of pace pace lead by you know, you becoming more certain to your tonality. They feel more certain as well That's far less powerful than the real way, which is through future pacing you allow someone To have the benefit now of imagining themselves in the future Using your product and feeling good That's emotional So, you know, they a car they're driving down the road Winding their hair top down feeling sexy feeling powerful loving life That's emotional certainty Imagine down the road having your broker so when you can trust build wealth over the long term and you ever tie it feeling great Knowing your money's working for you. That's emotional certainty knowing that your money's working for you making your money That's not going to work well until you believe it's so like believing it's so is logical certainty And imagining what it's like is emotional. So you get the difference believing.
